title: Right to File an FIR
summary: Every person has the legal right to register a First Information Report (FIR) for a cognizable offence at any police station.

plainExplanation.whatItMeans: If a serious crime has happened, the police must write down your complaint and start the legal process.
plainExplanation.whyItMatters: Without an FIR, police investigation usually cannot legally begin.

whoCanUseThisRight: Any person with knowledge of a cognizable offence
whenItApplies: The offence is cognizable (e.g., theft, assault, rape)
Information relates to a criminal act
whatPoliceMustDo: Provide a free copy of the FIR
commonViolations: Police ask victim to come later

exampleScenario.situation: A person reports a theft but police say it is not serious enough.
exampleScenario.explanation: Theft is a cognizable offence and FIR must be registered.

stepByStepAction: Go to the nearest police station
Ask for the FIR number and copy
Note the name of the officer

actions: Send written complaint to Superintendent of Police

mythsVsFacts.myth: Police can refuse FIR if the crime happened elsewhere
mythsVsFacts.fact: Zero FIR can be registered at any police station
